verdicts.reasoning: The $page variable from $_GET['page'] is checked against a whitelist via in_array() (line 362: array('loginpress-settings', 'loginpress', 'abw')). The redirect target is constructed via admin_url() with a hardcoded path. The $page_redirect is either 'loginpress' or 'loginpress-settings' (line 365). wp_safe_redirect validates the URL domain. No open redirect is possible. Additionally, current_user_can('manage_options') check at line 353 requires admin auth.

file_get_contents() reads from $_FILES['file']['tmp_name'] which is the PHP-assigned temporary file path for an uploaded file. The is_uploaded_file() check at line 199 validates this is a genuine upload. The tmp_name value is controlled by PHP, not the user. Requires admin auth (current_user_can('manage_options') at line 194) and nonce verification (check_ajax_referer at line 192). This is standard file upload handling.

$wpdb->insert() is used with explicit format specifiers ('%s', '%s', '%d', '%s', '%d', '%d') at line 305-316. WordPress's $wpdb->insert() method internally calls $wpdb->prepare() when format strings are provided, making this fully parameterized. All values are also pre-sanitized with sanitize_text_field() and absint(). Additionally requires admin auth and nonce verification.

The source is get_option('admin_email') which is not user-controlled input in the request. The wp_remote_post target URL is the LOGINPRESS_FEEDBACK_SERVER constant defined by the plugin, not user-supplied. The body data ($fields) contains sanitized site information. No SSRF is possible since the URL endpoint is hardcoded. Requires admin auth and nonce.

The input $_POST['src'] is cast to integer via absint() at line 540. The result is passed to wp_get_attachment_url() which returns a URL from the database or false. The output uses esc_url() at line 543 which is the correct escaping function for URL context. absint() alone eliminates any XSS vector since it produces only digits. Requires admin auth and nonce.

The video_id from $_POST['src'] is sanitized with sanitize_text_field() and inserted into a fixed URL pattern 'https://img.youtube.com/vi/' + $video_id + '/maxresdefault.jpg'. While sanitize_text_field doesn't strip path characters like '/', the domain is fixed to img.youtube.com. Path traversal within the YouTube domain does not constitute useful SSRF. The response body is never returned to the user (only the video_id is echoed). Requires admin auth and nonce. Admin users can already make arbitrary HTTP requests.

$_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] is sanitized via sanitize_text_field(), then wrapped in wp_nonce_url(), and output via esc_url(). esc_url() strips dangerous protocols and encodes special characters, making XSS impossible in URL context. This is rendered in an admin notice (admin_notices hook) which requires admin authentication (current_user_can('manage_options') check at line 74/90 in the parent function loginpress_review_notice).

Same pattern as loginpress-xss-002. $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] -> sanitize_text_field() -> wp_nonce_url() -> esc_url(). Output is in href attribute. esc_url() is the correct escaping for this context. Admin-only notice.
Same pattern as loginpress-xss-002. $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] -> sanitize_text_field() -> wp_nonce_url() -> esc_url(). Admin-only notice with proper escaping.

Same pattern as loginpress-xss-002 but in loginpress_addon_notice_text(). $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] -> sanitize_text_field() -> wp_nonce_url() -> esc_url(). Properly escaped for URL context in href attribute. Admin-only context.
Same pattern as loginpress-xss-002 but for Friday sale notice. $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] -> sanitize_text_field() -> wp_nonce_url() -> esc_url(). Properly escaped. Admin-only.

wp_safe_redirect() validates the redirect URL against the list of allowed hosts (wp_safe_redirect calls wp_validate_redirect which checks against allowed_redirect_hosts filter). The URL from $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] is just the path component with scheme changed to https. This is a standard HTTPS redirect pattern. wp_safe_redirect prevents redirection to external domains.

Same as loginpress-open-002. wp_safe_redirect() validates against allowed hosts. The URL is constructed from $_SERVER['HTTP_HOST'] and $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] with https:// prefix. While HTTP_HOST can be spoofed, wp_safe_redirect validates the domain against allowed hosts, preventing external redirects.

$_POST['redirect_to'] is sanitized with esc_url_raw() and then passed to wp_safe_redirect(). wp_safe_redirect validates the URL domain against allowed redirect hosts. External domains would be rejected. This is a standard WordPress redirect_to pattern used by core itself.

The output is: <input type="hidden" name="redirect_to" value="echo esc_url(wp_unslash($_GET['redirect_to']))">. esc_url() sanitizes the value by: (1) stripping dangerous protocols (javascript:, data:, etc.), (2) encoding special characters including quotes. Used in an HTML attribute value context, esc_url() is the correct escaping function since it encodes ampersands and double quotes. An attacker cannot break out of the value attribute or inject script.

The flagged code is: $dismissed = array_filter((array) get_user_meta($current_user->ID, '_rn_dismissed', true)). array_filter() without a callback simply removes falsy values from an array. It does not execute arbitrary code. get_user_meta returns stored metadata. There is no code execution vector here. The taint analyzer incorrectly classified array_filter as a code execution sink.

The flagged code is: $args = array_map('sanitize_text_field', wp_unslash($_GET)). array_map with a hardcoded callback string 'sanitize_text_field' applies that WordPress sanitization function to each element. The callback is not user-controlled. There is no code execution vector. This is actually a sanitization operation, not a dangerous sink.
Identical pattern to loginpress-rce-001. array_filter() on user meta is not a code execution sink. The function removes falsy values from an array.

The flagged code is: array_map('strval', $data['options']). The callback 'strval' is hardcoded and simply converts values to strings. The data source is get_option() which reads from the WordPress options table. Even if the option data were attacker-controlled (requiring prior admin access), array_map('strval', ...) does not execute arbitrary code. strval is a type-casting function.
Same pattern as loginpress-rce-004. array_map('strval', $data['user_meta']) with hardcoded 'strval' callback. No code execution possible.

Same pattern as loginpress-rce-004. array_map('strval', $rows) where $rows comes from a properly prepared $wpdb->get_col() query. The callback 'strval' is hardcoded. The database query uses $wpdb->prepare() with $wpdb->esc_like(). No code execution or SQL injection possible.

summary: All 21 taint flow findings in LoginPress are false positives. The plugin demonstrates generally solid security practices: (1) All AJAX handlers require admin authentication (current_user_can('manage_options')) and nonce verification. None are registered with nopriv. (2) REST API routes use proper permission_callback with manage_options capability check. (3) Database operations use $wpdb->insert() with format specifiers or $wpdb->prepare(). (4) Output escaping uses appropriate context-aware functions (esc_url for URLs, esc_attr for attributes, esc_html for text). (5) wp_safe_redirect prevents open redirects. (6) The RCE findings were entirely spurious - the taint analyzer incorrectly classified array_filter() and array_map() with hardcoded callbacks (strval, sanitize_text_field) as code execution sinks.
feedback: The taint analyzer has a significant issue with false-flagging array_map() and array_filter() as RCE sinks. These functions only execute arbitrary code when the callback parameter itself is user-controlled, which was not the case in any of these findings. The callbacks were all hardcoded strings ('strval', 'sanitize_text_field'). The XSS findings for esc_url() output are also false positives since esc_url() is the correct escaping function for URL context in HTML attributes. The open_redirect findings ignore that wp_safe_redirect() validates against allowed hosts. The SSRF finding on get_option('admin_email') incorrectly treated a database-stored value as a tainted source when the actual target URL was a hardcoded constant.
